words: This conversation with Pete Paolini took place at his home in Lewiston, Idaho
on October 21, 1976. The interviewer was Sam Schrager.
SAM SCHRAGER: What was it like there where you grew up in Florence that made
you want to leave?
PETE PAOLINI: I didn't grow up in the city, it was forty miles west of Florence.
About half way between Florence and the west coast. Just exactly half
way. Nice little town, they called it. We live amile
and a quarter out of town in a little acreage there. ^probably
eight or ten acres I would say. But we had the landlord then and that
was rough. You know, half went to the landlord. Everything we raise
and probably more sometimes.

speaker: SS
words: So you couldn't even make a living.

speaker: PP
words: No. No. It was really rough, and that's why my oldest brother,

speaker: PP
words: soon as he get out of the army, he came over. And then a sister and

speaker: PP
words: a brother follow him two year- no, I think the brother, he's over

speaker: PP
words: here in Wenatehee now, next to me, he left home, fourteen and a half

speaker: PP
words: years old. He came over to- what would you call a passport that

speaker: PP
words: wasn't his?

speaker: PP
words: Forged passport.

speaker: PP
words: Well, would it been somebody else? The guy was old enough and small

speaker: PP
words: so he took his name and passport and he came over. So when he apply

speaker: PP
words: for citizenship why he had to tell them the whole thing what happened,

speaker: PP
words: because he was anxious to come over but he couldn't; too young, you

speaker: PP
words: see, but^well developed and strong in you know.

speaker: PP
words: So they didn't punish him for that?

speaker: PP
words: Oh, no, no, no, they just made him go through the regular-

speaker: PP
words: Yeah, like anybody else. Take out the first paper. I think he was

speaker: PP
words: in Alaska when he became a citizen. He went to Alaska when the sawmill up in Elk River

speaker: PP
words: shut down and no hope of a steady job so he went

speaker: PP
words: to Alaska.

speaker: SS
words: So when these brothers and your sister came over, where did they go,

speaker: SS
words: when they first came to the states?

speaker: PP
words: Wilson Creek.

speaker: SS
words: Wilson Creek.

speaker: PP
words: Wilson Creek was a railroad town; lively little town; lot of work.

speaker: PP
words: It was a division point on the railroad. You know what I mean, you

speaker: PP
words: understand what I mean?

speaker: SS
words: Yes.

speaker: PP
words: Freight, not passengers. A division point for freight. All the freight

speaker: PP
words: in those days, they only travel fifteen, twenty miles an hour and lots

speaker: PP
words: of traffic on the railroad then. And when they got 110 miles out of

speaker: PP
words: Spokane they had their eight hours in.

speaker: SS
words: What did they do on the railroad? What were they doing?

speaker: PP
words: Some of they on the section. This brother I got over here now, he

speaker: PP
words: was a car inspector right in Wilson Creek. He pass on the freightthey

speaker: PP
words: all inspected, they wouldn't go by Wilson Creefyuntil all thewhat

speaker: PP
words: do you call the car? They call 'em hotboxes, you know when they

speaker: PP
words: start to get too hot, you know, they have to change the oil and then

speaker: PP
words: sometimes they change the plate. They don't make the freight car like

speaker: PP
words: they do now.

speaker: SS
words: Well, after the war there in Italy it was pretty rough, wasn't it?

speaker: SS
words: It was rough during the war, too. There ain't no question about it.

speaker: SS
words: Too much everything was rationed, you know. And even though

speaker: SS
words: we out there on an acreage, why, the landlord took his share

speaker: SS
words: first- (Chuckles)

speaker: SS
words: What did that mean, then, what did you eat mostly?

speaker: PP
words: Mostly what we call a pasta. You know it's made of such as

speaker: PP
words: macaroni and spaghetti and beans, lentil beans and greens.

speaker: SS
words: Did you have to go in the army there?

speaker: PP
words: Well, that's why I'm over here. Not only looking for work, but I was

speaker: PP
words: due to go in the army within eight or nine months. But, ah, boy, I

speaker: PP
words: didn't like that, I seen enough of I just didn't want to go into

speaker: PP
words: the army.

speaker: SS
words: I heard a lot of people that were going in were just going up to the

speaker: SS
words: lines and getting shot and—

speaker: PP
words: Well, what the hell, the army- just some ruler decided to go to war,

speaker: PP
words: why, that's all there is to it. In those days dis country you didn't

speaker: PP
words: have no draft, you know, just a volunteer army. It was late to go

speaker: PP
words: to get a passport, but I had a cousin that work for the county in the

speaker: PP
words: office, and he said, "I think I can get you through." And he told me

speaker: PP
words: just what to do, you know and sure enough they send me to the city

speaker: PP
words: twenty-five miles west- and you go to Spokane to pass the physical

speaker: PP
words: for the army. Well, over there was a city and he got me some paper ,

speaker: PP
words: got me started and I went and got a physical examination to see if I

speaker: PP
words: was fit to go to the army, before I was twenty-five I would come back,

speaker: PP
words: I signed an affidavit. And I passed the examination with kind of a

speaker: PP
words: doubt, guy told me what to do and fool the doctor; my

speaker: PP
words: heart.

speaker: SS
words: What did you do to do that?

speaker: PP
words: You know (breathes hard and fast).

speaker: SS
words: What did you do, jump around, run around?

speaker: PP
words: Yeah, and then if you started- breathes hard and fast again- your

speaker: PP
words: start beating faster, see. (Chuckles) Okay, you sign an affidavit

speaker: PP
words: before you're twenty-five you'd come back in and pass another examina

speaker: PP
words: tion, and see whether they let you stay over there. But I had a sis

speaker: PP
words: ter and three brothers by then over here, you see, and it made it a

speaker: PP
words: little easier. But unless I had this cousin working at the county,

speaker: PP
words: I wouldn't know what to do.

speaker: SS
words: So what year was this?

speaker: PP
words: 1920. In 1920 in April, yeah because by Easter we were out there in

speaker: PP
words: the middle of the ocean; Easter Sunday.

speaker: SS
words: Did you go with friends? Or by yourself?

speaker: PP
words: No, there were a few friends. Not only friends there was a girl,there

speaker: PP
words: that one of my brothers had gone with her, but he came out of the army

speaker: PP
words: too, and boy, he beat it! And then send for her and she was along..

speaker: SS
words: She was coming to marry him?

speaker: PP
words: Yeah, yeah. They got married the first day she got over here, got

speaker: PP
words: married in Ephrata. They had a nice little girl, you know, gee, I

speaker: PP
words: played with her a lot. But his wife didn't like it over here. After

speaker: PP
words: three years he had to go back over. The kid, it bother me to see that

speaker: PP
words: little baby go back. But when she was twenty, she married a guy and

speaker: PP
words: he had some relation in Pittsburgh and she decided to come over. And

speaker: PP
words: at twenty or twenty-one, I couldn't tell you which, but one of the two,

speaker: PP
words: either twenty or twenty-one, she had a decision to make whether she

speaker: PP
words: wanted to stay there and become an Italian citizen or come back here

speaker: PP
words: and be an American citizen. So, she come back and the immigration

speaker: PP
words: people wrote me quite a few times, they want lot information and I

speaker: PP
words: couldn't tell them too much, you know, after all my knowledge limited

speaker: PP
words: but finally I happened to think this kid was baptised in Ephrata. So

speaker: PP
words: I wrote to them and told them to write at the Ephrata County, you know

speaker: PP
words: and the record was still there.

speaker: SS
words: And it was okay then

speaker: PP
words: Yeah.

speaker: SS
words: Why didn't the mother like it here?

speaker: PP
words: Well, she couldn't talk. She didn't know anybody, just got disgusted

speaker: PP
words: right away. And it is kind of rough for a young

speaker: PP
words: hen you are young, you want to chase around and that was out.

speaker: SS
words: Were there other Italian women there that she could ... ?

speaker: PP
words: Yeah, my sister was there, you know, but that's all.

speaker: SS
words: That was it. Ive heard that most people that come over they left

speaker: SS
words: their wives behind or they were bachelors. Isn't that true?

speaker: PP
words: Well, of course, in those days, that was before your time, but there

speaker: PP
words: was a time when a guy could come over and work at least seven or eight

speaker: PP
words: months on the railroad and you had to tough her out. The railroad

speaker: PP
words: was kind of a rough business especially, you had some relation at

speaker: PP
words: the town, you live in a bunkhouse on a railroad car and do your own

speaker: PP
words: cook and your own washing and it's not very pleasant. But a lot of

speaker: PP
words: then those guys that did come over, and they either send their wife over

speaker: PP
words: to America, see.

speaker: SS
words: This living in a bunkhouse; that's what you did when you came over

speaker: SS
words: here.

speaker: PP
words: No, I didn't because I had a sister; she was well established and

speaker: PP
words: her husband had a pretty good job, too. And they had a nice home

speaker: PP
words: right in town, right in Wilson Creek, nice home, so I stayed there

speaker: PP
words: and she done the washing and I slept there and I ate there and paid

speaker: PP
words: room and board to them. But it was more like a home than live in a

speaker: PP
words: bunkhouse on the railroad. You never get to talk to anybody

speaker: PP
words: and you don't know anybody and no entertainment of any kind. You

speaker: PP
words: can't go and do anything. You know , yourself when you were twenty

speaker: PP
words: years old and live that kind of a life for two or three years, you'd

speaker: PP
words: get disgusted, wouldn't you?

speaker: SS
words: Yeah. That would be tough.

speaker: PP
words: Yeah, it would be.

speaker: SS
words: To be doing that- not to have anybody to cook for 'em. Like in the

speaker: SS
words: logging camps they always had a good cook.

speaker: PP
words: Logging camp, that's a different, I spent a year in the logging

speaker: PP
words: camp. I'm glad you mentioned that. They " the best, those

speaker: PP
words: lumberjack- I don't know about now, but in those days, the real lum

speaker: PP
words: berjack, they were good people. And play cards every night and cribbage-

speaker: PP
words: you know what that is, don't you? Play cribbage every night

speaker: PP
words: and talk. Everybody have a -some story you tell. Time went fast in

speaker: PP
words: a logging camp. the time I got into the logging camp- those

speaker: PP
words: camps, they was clean. You had the clean sheets every week, company

speaker: PP
words: furnished sheets, you know. You had the hot and cold water for shower.

speaker: PP
words: But two years before that, (Chuckles)the Wobblies got the woods on

speaker: PP
words: strike. And if it wasn't for the Wobblies -

speaker: SS
words: That's right, the Wobblies did-

speaker: PP
words: The Wobblies did- In fact, I have a neighbor over here, one of the

speaker: PP
words: best of friends over here, he's been a vice-president for the PFI,

speaker: PP
words: you know, this outfit here, he's one of my best friends. And he ad

speaker: PP
words: mits, you know. (Chuckles) The Wobblies is what made them clean out

speaker: PP
words: the camps. They were full of lice and everything else.

speaker: SS
words: That's what I've heard. I've heard the Wobblies did a good job.

speaker: PP
words: Oh, it was the Wobblies, it was the Wobblies that done it. No ques

speaker: PP
words: tion about it.

speaker: SS
words: What kind of a strike was it? In this area where you were, what was

speaker: SS
words: the camp that you were in?

speaker: SS
words: Elk River.

speaker: PP
words: Camp A up in Elk River. You know, out of Elk River.

speaker: SS
words: When they talked about that strike, did they say they shut everything

speaker: SS
words: down?

speaker: PP
words: Oh, yeah. Oh, you bet, you bet. The lumberjack usually they stick

speaker: PP
words: pretty much together. It isn't like- when we went on strike over here

speaker: PP
words: in the sawmill, you had so goddamn many scabs, that they finaly lost

speaker: PP
words: the strike. But thadlumberjacks— I've seen those guys- in those days

speaker: PP
words: I used to- you know, single, went to Spokane once in a while. Go

speaker: PP
words: down used to call^the skidway, Wobblies and all this, you know, but they would stop

speaker: PP
words: a guy, "I need a drink."

speaker: PP
words: And nobody ever refused them. Everybody would give 'em fifty cents.

speaker: PP
words: They were that kind of people.

speaker: SS
words: They took care of their own.

speaker: PP
words: You bet, you bet they did. In fact they were pretty well

speaker: PP
words: In order to get into Elk River you had to go to St. Maries on the Mil

speaker: PP
words: waukee, you know, and then from St. Maries, you took the little branch

speaker: PP
words: line or the passenger train that went into Elk River, I think right

speaker: PP
words: around sixty-five, seventy miles, I don't know. They made a run into

speaker: PP
words: Elk River and . But that conductor there, the only conductor I

speaker: PP
words: know, and the lumberjacks come in from Spokane going up to Elk River

speaker: PP
words: or stop at Bovill or Clarkia. They knew each other from a hundred

speaker: PP
words: miles around. And they didn't have any , no money, but up there

speaker: PP
words: the conductor would never throw 'em out. But he said h e never lost

speaker: PP
words: a cent. You bet, and something else. I put a year in there

speaker: PP
words: and the trouble was that when I got ready to go back to work, to the

speaker: PP
words: camps, they were still shut down and I wasn't used to loafing around

speaker: PP
words: so I went to work in the sawmill. But otherwise, I want to go back

speaker: PP
words: in the woods so bad. You get so that you like that kind of work. You

speaker: PP
words: like the people.

speaker: SS
words: The camps were shut down because of the weather or because of the

speaker: SS
words: strike?

speaker: PP
words: Yeah. I've seen in those days, course in those days you had a horse.

speaker: PP
words: You didn t have  you have today. Everything was a crosscut and

speaker: PP
words: a horse to skid your logs, you know, and it got too soft. I've seen

speaker: PP
words: the time that you went into Spokane looking for a skiner, they used

speaker: PP
words: to call him; two sawyers; four sawyers job to do after the Fourth.

speaker: PP
words: Yes, many, many places it was still wet up til the Fourth.

speaker: SS
words: Short season.

speaker: PP
words: Yeah, it was season work, that's what it was. That's why the lumber

speaker: PP
words: jack, you never found one that had a wife, you know, they were all

speaker: PP
words: single guys. They went to Spokane with a couple hundred bucks-two

speaker: PP
words: hundred bucks in day was a lot of money, two three hundred bucks,

speaker: PP
words: in about a week or two week's time come back broke.

speaker: SS
words: And drink. Drink, right?

speaker: PP
words: Oh, yeah. Moonshine! In those days nothing but moonshine. (Chuckles)

speaker: SS
words: And then there were all the prostitutes.

speaker: PP
words: Oh, yeah, right. Oh, yeah. And some of those smart gals they would

speaker: PP
words: get ahold of guy they'd stay with him, you know, and probably took

speaker: PP
words: his money away and say,. because he'll get drunk. And some of those

speaker: PP
words: prostitutes they'd be honest enough, you know, to, not to gyp him too

speaker: PP
words: much. But they got pretty well paid.

speaker: SS
words: These guys though- it's a funny thing about- One thing, you look back

speaker: SS
words: at it, you know- It seems that when you blow in- it meant a guy'd

speaker: SS
words: make a lot of money and then it's all gone. Right? Then start from

speaker: SS
words: scratch again.

speaker: PP
words: You don't have a wife, you don't have a family, no responsibility, no

speaker: PP
words: nothin', and it gets so you've been broke once or twice, but you

speaker: PP
words: never suffer because somebody carry you along. See. You done the

speaker: PP
words: same thing for somebody else. It's unbelieveable. But the lumber

speaker: PP
words: jack, they stuck together and they understood it. If a guy was

speaker: PP
words: drunk they knew how he went broke, see.

speaker: SS
words: Did you have to know the guy very good?

speaker: PP
words: A lumberjack—»You could spot a lumberjack a mile away.(Laughs)

speaker: SS
words: How was that?

speaker: PP
words: Oh, yeah.

speaker: SS
words: How can you spot 'em? They way he was dressed?

speaker: PP
words: They way they talk, you know. They all had that dirty word in their

speaker: PP
words: mouth, you know. Never open their mouth unless they said, "Dammit!"

speaker: PP
words: or, "Fuck you." Or nonsense like that! That's the way they talk.

speaker: PP
words: It was no place for a lady to be around. See.

speaker: SS
words: Did you really like the class of people that were lumberjacks better

speaker: SS
words: then the guys that were at the sawmills?

speaker: PP
words: Yes, I did, I did. There's no question about it. I could tell you

speaker: PP
words: that I've been beat out of a few hundred, quite a few, too, but never

speaker: PP
words: from a lumberjack.

speaker: SS
words: What's the difference like between those guys and the sawmillers?

speaker: SS
words: The guys in the sawmill at Elk River?

speaker: PP
words: Well, the sawmill, it's different. It's a different breed of a people,

speaker: PP
words: The sawmill; you have a family. You go home, you know, to your family

speaker: PP
words: every day, but the lumberjack's a different guy, see. He didn't have

speaker: PP
words: responsibility. And that's why they live different. But they

speaker: PP
words: were honest.

speaker: SS
words: What did you do when you were working in the woods as a lumberjack?

speaker: PP
words: When I went in the woods I saw. Saw, gyppo, see. So much a thousand.

speaker: SS
words: That was you and your brother?

speaker: PP
words: And my brother, yeah. Then you see in those days, what we do, we

speaker: PP
words: went to any camp, if they didn't have a job right then, they'll

speaker: PP
words: hold you to stay in and they find somebody, you know. And most of

speaker: PP
words: the time it take two or four guys to what they call a strip of

speaker: PP
words: timber to log that off. Bring them down to the railroad. In those

speaker: PP
words: days, like I say, they logged different. They built the railroad

speaker: PP
words: into the woods. Like I said, when we went up in the woods, you

speaker: PP
words: know, we went in there and the foreman said they wait a day or two

speaker: PP
words: We ate in there. And sure enough it was only a day and

speaker: PP
words: a half or so and here comes a couple guys. So the four would go to

speaker: PP
words: gether. One was what they call a swamper, and one the skinner, see,

speaker: PP
words: and I and my brother were the sawyers. We saw the logs in teen

speaker: PP
words: foot length. And the swamper would see that the guy with the team,

speaker: PP
words: have a load ready for him to take along. When we got half way through

speaker: PP
words: this- our job, those guys, they were both Frenchmen, and they " a

speaker: PP
words: fight among themselves, so one pull off and went. And we had so

speaker: PP
words: damn much money accumulated- because we were getting just the wage,

speaker: PP
words: I think it was $3.40, $3.50 a day-

speaker: SS
words: And you get a bonus.

speaker: PP
words: And the bonus. That was cash you see, but we had to finish our job

speaker: PP
words: in order to collect that. The strip was. so much a thousand.

speaker: SS
words: And you get the bonus on every thousand, right?

speaker: PP
words: Bonus on every thousand at the end of the job. The end of the job

speaker: PP
words: the foreman would come out and look the strip over and see if it was

speaker: PP
words: okay, and then they pay you off.

speaker: SS
words: So what happened when they left?

speaker: PP
words: Then we handle between the three of us, you see. If they get some

speaker: PP
words: body else in probably that guy would have give him $150- 200 bucks.

speaker: PP
words: So we done it ourselves.

speaker: SS
words: You handled it, the three of you?

speaker: PP
words: The three of us, yeah.

speaker: SS
words: Could you handle it?

speaker: PP
words: Well, we did this way In order to fix the road, the skid road,

speaker: PP
words: sometime we go out after supper, see, and do the little bit of a jobs.

speaker: PP
words: Because they won't let you keep any team out, you know, after eight

speaker: PP
words: hours. But you could go out and fix your trail. Or if you had a

speaker: PP
words: bad tree that was leaning in a different way and it took you a long

speaker: PP
words: time to do just what you wanted.

speaker: SS
words: You'd do that after supper?

speaker: PP
words: Yeah..

speaker: SS
words: So did you have to work really hard on that strip?

speaker: PP
words: It was hard until you got onto it. I was new. Just a young punk of

speaker: PP
words: a kid, you know. But stayed with it and we done it, in fact we

speaker: PP
words: built a little reputation for doing it.

speaker: SS
words: You mean for doing it- just the three of you doing it?

speaker: PP
words: Um-huh.

speaker: SS
words: Did you join the IWW when you came there?

speaker: PP
words: No, no. In fact. They didn't bother you too much then, either. If

speaker: PP
words: it had come around, But by then they didn't bother you. The IWW, they

speaker: PP
words: pulled that strike then that was it. And I don't know, I couldn't

speaker: PP
words: tell you too much about that because, just like I said, they didn't

speaker: PP
words: bother us. And I kind of think they left you alone for a year or so.

speaker: PP
words: Join the IWW, they want to be sure that you meant it. You know and

speaker: PP
words: stay with it. I kind of think, that's the way I got it.

speaker: SS
words: Now what year was it that you were in the woods there?

speaker: SS
words: Okay, well, going back a little before that; when you first came

speaker: SS
words: When you first came here from Italy; when you first got out there,

speaker: SS
words: how did you go, did you take the train all the way out?

speaker: PP
words: Yes, I did. In fact I stopped in Chicago. Course, you know by that

speaker: PP
words: time I was told just what to do, writing to my brother, and he had

speaker: PP
words: grown kids- he had a widow. And he told me what to do, so

speaker: PP
words: I stopped in Chicago and some people there and they found a guy

speaker: PP
words: that was coming into Spokane. So I went into St. Paul with this guy

speaker: PP
words: and the railroad give me a pass to come out West. That was pretty nice

speaker: PP
words: but they done it right along.. They did want to build the West anyhow.

speaker: PP
words: Beside I knew I couldn't go out and do anything else by railroad, see.

speaker: PP
words: And I did. I went out on the railroad.

speaker: SS
words: How come you knew railroad was the only job you could do?

speaker: PP
words: Well, what else could you do when you can't talk? And who would hire

speaker: PP
words: you? See, even in the sawmill, unless you know, you know, can talk

speaker: PP
words: a little bit so the foreman can But on the railroad,

speaker: PP
words: just one job and after one day or two you're on your own.

speaker: SS
words: But you'd never done that back in Italy? Railroads?

speaker: PP
words: Oh, no, no.

speaker: SS
words: So what was it like with the language? To not know English. Was

speaker: SS
words: that hard at first?

speaker: PP
words: Well, yeah. But what I mean, but not to work, because like I said,

speaker: PP
words: it's a job that you learn it so quick, you see, that most particular

speaker: PP
words: job was it-

speaker: SS
words: What was it? What was it that you did on that job?

speaker: PP
words: he marked the bad one to pull out and you placed th e new

speaker: PP
words: one. See, the track you know what you do there.

speaker: SS
words: Were most of the guys on that crew Italian?

speaker: PP
words: No. NO.

speaker: SS
words: Just you?

speaker: SS
words: What do you mean, you couldn't buy your own cigarettes?

speaker: PP
words: Well, gosh dammit, I finally got so that I listened to them guys, you

speaker: PP
words: know, And and so, cigarrette, cigarrette. (Chuckles)

speaker: PP
words: But they would say, "What kind?" Well, I had an idea what they said,

speaker: PP
words: and in those days, it was Camel, Chesterfield and Lucky Strike; you

speaker: PP
words: very seldom see any other brand.

speaker: SS
words: What about the work itself? Was it hard? Was it real hard work?

speaker: PP
